text: The surgeon performs hysteropexy with a transparietal suture to achieve wider exposure of the pelvis. Non-electrified scissors are used for careful dissection of the pouch from surrounding tissues, ensuring complete liberation. Sharp dissection of the anterior surface of the pouch is conducted to separate it from the uterus, taking care to avoid inadvertent enterotomies or damage to the pouch wall.

The surgeon uncovers the spiral course of the pouch on its longitudinal axis and completes the mobilization of the J-pouch using monopolar curved scissors. The dissection progresses down to the anastomotic level, where non-electrified scissors are used to take down the ileal pouch anal anastomosis. A fingertip aids in identifying the anastomotic borders, ensuring the pouch is fully mobilized from the pelvis.

The surgeon mobilizes the mesentery from the retroperitoneum and the superior mesenteric axis up to its origin to derotate the pouch. A suture is placed at the apex of the pouch, and the bowel is transposed to the pelvis, ensuring correct orientation. ICG fluorescence angiography is used to confirm optimal vascular perfusion, visible on the seromuscular surface.

The surgeon performs a redo ileal pouch anal anastomosis transanally. A long star retractor is used to expose the upper anal canal. Singular interrupted sutures are placed at cardinal points, followed by additional stitches along the circumferential margin to complete the anastomosis. Full thickness bites are carefully taken to avoid including the posterior vaginal wall or external sphincter. ICG is used to confirm valid perfusion of the ileal and rectal mucosa.
